name: MvvmState
tagline: A notes screen that teaches MVVM: ViewModel + StateFlow + immutable UiState + unidirectional data flow.
whatItDoes: You see a 'Notes' screen with a 'New note' text field, an 'Add' button, and a scrolling list of notes. On launch a spinner shows for a moment ('Loading notes...') while a fake repository simulates I/O, then three seeded notes appear, one already struck through because it is marked done. You can type a title and tap Add to append a note, tap a checkbox to toggle a note done (strike-through), or tap Delete to remove one. The whole screen always reflects a single immutable snapshot of state held in the ViewModel.
mentalModel: State flows DOWN to the UI and events flow UP to one onEvent funnel; the ViewModel owns the only mutable copy of state and survives rotation, so the UI is just a pure drawing of whatever NotesUiState currently is.

screens.name: MainActivity
screens.role: The single Activity; installs the Compose tree, applies MvvmStateTheme, and wraps the screen in a Scaffold.

screens.name: NotesScreen
screens.role: The STATEFUL screen: obtains the NotesViewModel, collects its uiState, and forwards events to onEvent.

screens.name: NotesScreenContent
screens.role: The STATELESS screen: a pure function of a NotesUiState plus an onEvent sink that draws the title field, Add button, and the loading spinner or note list.

screens.name: NoteRow
screens.role: Renders one Note with a done-checkbox (strike-through when done) and a Delete button, reporting taps via callbacks.

concepts.plain: NotesViewModel extends androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel, a holder that the framework keeps alive across configuration changes like rotation, so the state it owns is not destroyed and recreated with the Activity.

NotesUiState is a data class whose every property (notes, isLoading, newNoteTitle) is a 'val'. It is never edited in place; the ViewModel always builds a brand-new instance with .copy(...) so the screen always renders one consistent snapshot.

The ViewModel keeps a private writable MutableStateFlow named _uiState and exposes it publicly only as a read-only StateFlow named uiState via asStateFlow(). The UI can read but never write the state, which enforces one-way data flow.

In NotesScreen, 'val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()' subscribes to the StateFlow, turns each emission into Compose State so the screen recomposes, and pauses collecting while the screen is not visible.

NotesEvent is a sealed interface listing every user intention (NewTitleChanged, AddNote, ToggleDone, DeleteNote). Because it is sealed, the 'when (event)' in onEvent is exhaustive at compile time and needs no 'else'.
State flows DOWN (uiState -> composables) and events flow UP (callbacks -> onEvent). The UI never mutates state; it only sends a NotesEvent, the ViewModel computes a new state, and Compose redraws.

Async work runs in viewModelScope.launch and calls suspend functions on NotesRepository (loadNotes, addNote, toggleDone, deleteNote). The scope is cancelled automatically when the ViewModel is cleared, so coroutines never leak.
NotesScreenContent holds no ViewModel and no remembered state, so the @Preview functions can hand it a fabricated NotesUiState and a no-op onEvent ({}) without constructing a ViewModel.

files.role: The UI layer: the Activity, the stateful NotesScreen, the stateless NotesScreenContent, the NoteRow, and the @Preview functions.
The data layer: the immutable Note model and a fake in-memory repository whose suspend functions add a delay() to mimic disk/network latency.
The heart of the lesson: the immutable NotesUiState, the sealed NotesEvent interface, and NotesViewModel exposing uiState plus the single onEvent funnel.
The student-facing overview of the MVVM/UDF lesson, the key files, what to inspect, the rotation experiment, and run commands.

highlights.title: The immutable UiState snapshot
highlights.explain: NotesUiState bundles everything the screen needs (notes, isLoading, newNoteTitle) into ONE data class where every field is a 'val'. Because it is immutable it is never edited in place; the ViewModel replaces it wholesale with .copy(...). Bundling the fields together means the UI always sees a consistent snapshot with no half-updated window.

highlights.title: Sealed events: the fixed vocabulary of user intent
highlights.explain: NotesEvent is a sealed interface naming every action the UI may request. Data classes like ToggleDone(val id: Int) carry payloads, while data object AddNote carries none. Because the set is sealed, the compiler knows it is complete, so the 'when' over it can be exhaustive and adding a new event forces you to handle it.

highlights.title: Private writable vs public read-only state
highlights.explain: _uiState is a private MutableStateFlow seeded in the isLoading=true state, and uiState exposes it as a read-only StateFlow via asStateFlow(). This split is what physically enforces 'state down, events up': the UI can observe uiState but cannot write it. The init{} block kicks off the first loadNotes() once when the ViewModel is created, not on every rotation.

highlights.title: onEvent: the single funnel for all state changes
highlights.explain: onEvent is the ONLY place state mutates. The exhaustive 'when (event)' routes each NotesEvent to a state update: NewTitleChanged does a synchronous _uiState.update { it.copy(newNoteTitle = ...) }, while AddNote/ToggleDone/DeleteNote delegate to private coroutine helpers. Trace any UI action to exactly one branch here.

highlights.title: Async load: flip isLoading, await, emit a new state
highlights.explain: loadNotes runs inside viewModelScope.launch so the suspend repository.loadNotes() call never blocks the UI thread. The pattern to memorize: update to isLoading=true (spinner shows), await the data, then update with notes=loaded and isLoading=false. Each step is a fresh immutable NotesUiState built with copy().

highlights.title: Stateful screen: wiring state DOWN and events UP
highlights.explain: NotesScreen is the one Composable that knows about the ViewModel. viewModel() returns one scoped to the host so it survives rotation; collectAsStateWithLifecycle() reads uiState as Compose State (state DOWN); and onEvent = viewModel::onEvent is a method reference passing every action UP. No business logic lives in the composable.

highlights.title: Stateless content driven entirely by uiState
highlights.explain: NotesScreenContent takes only a NotesUiState and an onEvent lambda. The OutlinedTextField renders uiState.newNoteTitle and fires NotesEvent.NewTitleChanged on every keystroke; the field keeps NO internal text of its own, so the ViewModel is the single source of truth. Tapping Add just emits NotesEvent.AddNote rather than building a Note itself.

newVsPrev: NetworkParsing, WebSocketLive and LocationServices all moved data on and off the main thread with suspend repositories. MvvmState keeps that async/suspend habit (a fake suspend repository with delay) but adds the full MVVM architecture on top: a retained ViewModel that owns an immutable UiState, exposes it as a read-only StateFlow, and accepts a sealed set of UI events through one onEvent funnel. The new ideas here are unidirectional data flow, state surviving rotation in the ViewModel, splitting the UI into a stateful holder plus a stateless previewable Composable, and collectAsStateWithLifecycle() bridging the flow to Compose.
tryThis: Add a new event: declare 'data object ClearAll : NotesEvent', wire a button in NotesScreenContent that calls onEvent(NotesEvent.ClearAll), and watch the compiler force you to add a branch in onEvent before it will build (proof that the sealed 'when' is exhaustive).

Do the rotation experiment from the README: run the app, type into 'New note' but do NOT tap Add, then rotate the emulator. The draft text and list survive because the state lives in the retained NotesViewModel.

Break unidirectional flow on purpose: give OutlinedTextField a local 'var' via remember instead of reading uiState.newNoteTitle, then rotate, and observe the draft now gets lost, demonstrating why state belongs in the ViewModel.
Increase the delay(600) in NotesRepository.loadNotes() to delay(3000) and run the app to clearly see the CircularProgressIndicator driven by the single isLoading flag.
Add a third @Preview that passes NotesUiState(notes = emptyList(), isLoading = false) and confirm the empty list renders without ever constructing a ViewModel.
gotchas: NotesRepository is FAKE and in-memory: there is no Room database and no Retrofit. The delay() calls only simulate latency, and the seeded notes reset every time the app process restarts because nothing is actually persisted.

Mutating actions re-read the whole list after every change: addNote, toggleDone, and deleteNote each call repository.loadNotes() again rather than patching uiState locally. That is a teaching simplification; a real app would usually update the in-memory state directly to avoid the extra round trip.

The @Preview functions deliberately render NotesScreenContent (the stateless overload) with a hand-built state and onEvent = {}; they never build a NotesViewModel, because a preview has no real lifecycle or coroutine environment and would also trigger the init{} loading coroutine.

The repository's backing list is a private mutableListOf, but loadNotes() returns notes.toList() (a defensive copy) so callers can never mutate the internal store; the authoritative state still lives only in the ViewModel.
runTest: Open the project in Android Studio and Run, or from the project root build and install with ./gradlew installDebug (or ./gradlew assembleDebug to just build the APK). On launch watch for the brief 'Loading notes...' spinner, then the three seeded notes with 'Read about ViewModel' struck through. Type a title and tap Add to append a note, tap a checkbox to toggle strike-through, and tap Delete to remove one. Then type into 'New note' (do not tap Add) and rotate the emulator to confirm the draft and list survive. Run the unit tests with ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest to see that .copy(...) produces a new value without mutating the original.
